title: The Wreck the Storm Woke

giver.name: Rook Adair's last sounding-chart
giver.wound: Rook charted the deep shoals for a merchant house that went under with the sea, and marked one wreck 'safe only in stillness' — a warning she underlined twice. She drowned the same week she finished the chart, checking her own soundings a final time in weather she should have waited out.

steps.text: The chart surfaces in a salvage-crate after the last great storm, its ink still sharp under decades of wax sealing. One wreck is marked with Rook's underlined warning — 'safe only in stillness' — and a fresh gap in the flats nearby shows the storm has finally torn open the ground that used to bury it.

You reach the exposed wreck: an old storm-hunter's vessel, hull split along an old strike-scar, its hold spilled open onto the salt. Inside, lashed down against exactly this kind of exposure, are crates of storm-glass, still catching light like the lightning that made them is somehow still inside.

Rook's warning wasn't about the storm that sank the ship — it was about the storm-glass itself. A logbook page wedged in the crates explains: this crew was harvesting glass deliberately, mid-storm, chasing bigger and bigger strikes for a buyer who paid by the size of the fused chunk. The last entry, in a different, shakier hand than the rest: 'we should have stopped three storms ago. It isn't the lightning I'm afraid of anymore.' Whatever they were chasing, they never got to name.

options.text: Take the glass but leave the logbook and the wreck itself undisturbed as a warning marker — mark it on your own charts the way Rook marked hers.

ledger.note: Maren respects a warning heeded, not just glass collected

options.text: Strip the wreck completely — glass, fittings, everything salvageable. A warning from a dead crew isn't a reason to leave good salvage on the flats.

ledger.note: practical, and a little deaf to what the warning cost the people who wrote it

steps.text: You take the crated glass and leave the hull marked with a stone cairn, the same shorthand Rook used for danger. Salvagers passing after you give it a wider berth than they would an ordinary wreck, without quite knowing why.

You strip the wreck to the ribs — timber, fittings, every last shard of glass. It's a good haul. The flats feel a little more ordinary afterward, the warning stripped away along with everything else worth taking.

echo.text: Mark: the cairn becomes a known landmark, and storm-chasers who hear the logbook's story start rationing how hard they push a storm for glass — a small, real check on greed the wreck itself couldn't provide while it was buried. Strip: the wreck vanishes into the village's stores within a week, and the next great storm sees at least one salvager push further into the lightning than they should, chasing the same kind of haul.

curatorNote: Covers the chart-to-storm-exposed-wreck ask, mirroring calling-chart-leads-deep's giver-as-document pattern. Turn: the old crew's warning was about their own greed chasing bigger strikes, not the storm itself — echoes the storm-glass-collector quest's theme from a different angle without repeating it.
